coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots for temperature probe and the detection of tetracycline based on the inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chunxi
Jiao, Yang
Gao, Zhe
Yang, Yaling
Li, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S co-doped CDs) were prepared from glutathione with a hydrothermal method and developed for temperature probe as well as fluorescent sensor of tetracycline. The as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs exhibited good optical properties, stability, water solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield(17.5%). The fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs varied with temperature in the range of 10–70 °C. Moreover, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the nanoparticle-based sensor displayed a wide linear range of 0.1–65 μM (R2 = 0.9916) and provided an exciting detection limit of 0.04 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) for quantifying tetracycline. More strikingly, the N, S co-doped CDs sensing system could commendably resist the interference from potential foreign substances, and achieved satisfactory recoveries, which indicated the excellent selectivity and sufficient accuracy of the sensor for real milk samples analyses. As a simple and effective optical nanosensor, the proposed N, S co-doped CDs hold admirable promise to broaden the applications in food analysis and others fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S co-doped carbon dots
Tetracycline
Fluorescence quenching
Inner filter effect
Temperature probe

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs
Characterization and fluorescence of the N, S co-doped CDs
Performance of the N, S co-doped CDs for temperature sensing
Analytical performance of the N, S co-doped CDs for TC detection
Detection of TC in milk samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The processes of synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs, temperature sensing and possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching by TC.
(a) The TEM imaging and (b)Size distribution of the S, B co-doped CDs.
The (a)XRD; (b)FTIR; (c)Full-scan XPS spectrum of the N, S co-doped CDs.

(a)UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of the N, S co-doped CDs; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 290 to 350 nm in 10 nm increments.

(a) F/F0-temperature plots of N,S co-doped CDs during heating and cooling processes. (inset: photographs of N,S co-doped CDs under different temperatures and fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs at different temperatures); (b) Reversible temperature-dependence of the fluorescence of the N, S co-doped CDs.

The effect of (a)pH; (b) Incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of TC (40 μM) to the N, S co-doped CDs; (c) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of TC(Inset: fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs with different concentrations of TC); (d) The linear plot of F/F0 and a series of concentrations of TC(inset: photographs of N,S co-doped CDs with addition of different concentrations of TC under daylight and UV irradiation).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs) of TC, fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of the N, S co-doped CDs; (b) Fluorescence emission decay curve of CDs and CDs-TC.
Effect of varies of interfering substances on the detection of TC (125 μM).
Comparison of different methods for the detection of TC.
Determination and spiked recoveries for milk samples.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots for temperature probe and the detection of tetracycline based on the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Product processing, Yunnan Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650033, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and environmental engineering, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA 15213, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and environmental engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S co-doped CDs) were prepared from glutathione with a hydrothermal method and developed for temperature probe as well as fluorescent sensor of tetracycline. The as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs exhibited good optical properties, stability, water solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield(17.5%). The fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs varied with temperature in the range of 10–70 °C. Moreover, based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the nanoparticle-based sensor displayed a wide linear range of 0.1–65 μM (R2 = 0.9916) and provided an exciting detection limit of 0.04 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) for quantifying tetracycline. More strikingly, the N, S co-doped CDs sensing system could commendably resist the interference from potential foreign substances, and achieved satisfactory recoveries, which indicated the excellent selectivity and sufficient accuracy of the sensor for real milk samples analyses. As a simple and effective optical nanosensor, the proposed N, S co-doped CDs hold admirable promise to broaden the applications in food analysis and others fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) (Fig. 1 ), as one of antibiotics, it was a broad spectrum antibiotic which could against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. Due to the broad antibacterial spectrum, TC was used in aquaculture and veterinary medicines widely to prevent and treat infections and promote growth [1,2]. However, excessive use of TC would lead it to accumulate in animals and even migrates to foods from animals, such as milk and meat [3]. Generally, the concentrations of antibiotic residues in body are in low level, but it could lead to the resistance of bacterial to antibiotics in long-term. The result would be harmful to animals and even humans. Therefore, it is especially necessary to detect the TC in the animal products. Up to now, varies of methods have been developed to detect TC. These methods include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–6], electrochemical method [7,8], spectroscopic techniques [9–11]. Compared with other methods which are expensive and time-consuming, the fluorescence spectrophotometry avoided above disadvantages and obtained more and more attention.

Temperature as one of basic physical parameters plays an important role in chemical and biological systems. In many cases, high or low temperatures could have great influence on the system. Therefore, it is necessary to achieve the accurate determination of temperature. In recent years, some fluorescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, polymers and organic dyes were applied as temperature sensors [12–14]. However, it is also challenging for their cytotoxicity, tedious probe preparation procedures and poor photostability. Recently, many studies have reported that carbon dots could be designed as temperature probes and attracted more and more attention [15,16].

Carbon dots (CDs), as one kind of carbon materials, usually have size of less than 10 nm [17]. In recent years, CDs have attracted more and more interests owing to their excellent properties, such as fluorescence characteristics, low toxicity, good stability, water solubility and biocompatibility [18–21]. Based on these excellent properties, CDs were applied in different fields including detection, cell imaging, pH and temperature sensors [22–25]. To date, different kinds of materials that involved natural materials and synthetic compounds have been used as carbon sources for synthesizing CDs [26–29]. Recently, some literatures indicated that the CDs doped with heteroatoms had higher quantum yields and showed different spectral properties, electronic energy levels and local chemical properties from common CDs which only contained C, H as well as O [22,30,31]. Therefore, great efforts were made to develop heteroatom-doped CDs for improving the optical and electrical properties of CDs. In various heteroatoms, N, S, B, P were commonly doped in CDs [32–35].

In this work, we proposed a novel one-step hydrothermal route to fabricate N, S co-doped CDs by using glutathione as precursors without any additional surface modification. The glutathione was chosen to synthesize N, S co-doped CDs for it could provide carbon source, nitrogen source and sulfur source, simultaneously. N and S atoms can be doped into the CDs at the same time and the other sources of N and S are not needed any more which effectively avoids the soak of the other impurities. The as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characteristics, stability and good temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response from 10 to 70 °C. More importantly, the fluorescence of the N, S co-doped CDs could be quenched by TC selectively and sensitively. Based on inner filter effect (IFE), which results from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light of fluorophores by absorbers in the detection system, the nanoparticle-based sensor was used to detect the TC in the milk samples. Results indicated that the excellent selectivity and sufficient accuracy of the sensor for real milk samples analyses. The process of temperature sensing and detecting TC by the prepared N, S co-doped CDs was shown in Fig. 2 .

Glutathione(GSH), tetracycline hydrochloride(TC), ascorbic acid(AA) were provided by Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Gentamicin, roxithromycin, azithromycin, penicillin, clarithromycin, amoxicillin were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd.(shanghai, China). Leucine (Leu), Alanine (Ala), Tyrosine (Tyr), Cystine (Cys), Glutamic acid (Glu), Aspartic Acid (Asp) and Tryptophane (Try) were obtained from Xinxing Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, CuSO4, ZnSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, Na2HPO4, NaCl were purchased from were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification.

UV-vis absorption spectra was obtained on a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was carried out on a TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra was recorded a G9800 A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope(FEI, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, Germany). A SK-1 vortex mixer and a PHSJ-4 A pH meter were used in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S co-doped CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1.5 g glutathione was dissolved in 100 mL distilled water, and the mixture was then treated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, N, S co-doped CDs were obtained after centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and filtration with a 0.22 μm membrane. The as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The quantum yield of the N, S co-doped CDs was measured according to the previous reports [36,37]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 excited at 360 nm) was chosen as a reference. Emission spectra of both the sample and reference were recorded at the same excitation at 360 nm. The values of the quantum yield were calculated according to the following equation:
Q = QR × (IS / IR) × (AR/AS) × (ηS/ηR) 2
Where “Q” is quantum yield, “I” is emission intensity at the excitation wavelength, “A” and “η” are the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and the solvent refraction index, respectively. The subscript “R” refers to reference standard(quinine sulfate) and “S” refers to the samples.

The milk bought from local supermarket and treated according to previous report [38]. In brief, 4 mL milk was put into a centrifuge tube and diluted to 10 mL. After added with 2 mL of trichloroacetic acid(10%) and 2 mL of chloroform, the mixture was vortexed for 1 min. Then, the mixture was treated with 15 min of sonication at 20 °C and 10 min of centrifugation at 13,000 rpm. The supernatant was centrifuged again for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. The final solution was used for detection of TC.

200 μL of N, S co-doped CDs were added into a 10 mL centrifuge tubes and diluted to 3 mL. Then, added with 500 μL Na2PO4-ascorbic acid buffer solution. After different concentration of TC or samples were added into centrifuge tubes, the mixture was vortexed for 5 s and incubated for 1 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and an emission wavelength of 440 nm with 5 nm slits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and fluorescence of the N, S co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.3 a showed the TEM of the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs. It could be observed that the N, S co-doped CDs dispersed well and were spherical shape with an average diameter of 3 nm(Fig.3b). Fig. 4 a showed the XRD of the N, S co-doped CDs. It showed a broad diffraction peak at 24.3°, which is attributed to their graphitic components [39]. FTIR and XPS were applied to analyze functional groups on the surface of the CDs. FTIR was shown in Fig. 4b. The broad peak at about 3404 cm−1 is attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibration. The peaks at 2929 cm−1 was assigned to the C–H stretching vibration, while the small peak at 2302 cm−1 corresponded to SH bond. Peaks located at 1656 cm−1, 1564 cm−1, 1382 cm−1, 1151 cm−1 and 1031 cm−1 were verified as stretching vibrations of CO, CC, C–N, C–S and C–O, respectively. Based on the above FTIR analysis, the results indicated the there were −OH, −COOH, −NH2 and −SH on the surface of the N, S co-doped CDs. XPS was also used to characterize the surface elemental composition and function groups of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, the full scan XPS spectrum of the N, S co-doped CDs showed four peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, 532 eV and 164 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s displayed six main peaks(Fig. S1a). The peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.0 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.2 eV were attributed to CC, C–H, C–S, C–N, C–O and CO, respectively. For the N1s high-resolution spectrum, as shown in Fig. S1b, there were two peaks were observed at 399.4 eV and 401.2 eV, which attributed to C–N and NH. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s in Fig. S1c showed two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.4 eV, corresponding to CO and CO. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p was shown in Fig. S1d.There were two peaks at 163.2 eV and 164.9 eV, which were assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the CS covalent bond, respectively [40]. The results agree with which got from analysis of FTIR. Therefore, the surfaces of the as-prepared CDs doped with N, S were rich in −COOH, −OH, −NH2 and −SH.

The as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs exhibit excellent optical properties. Fig. 5 a showed the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra showed a broad absorption, which was assigned to the n-π* transition of N, S co-doped CDs [31]. In addition, a strong emission with the maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm could be observed under excitation at 320 nm. The synthesized N, S co-doped CDs were light yellow under visible light, while very bright blue luminescence was seen under the UV (365 nm) light in aqueous solution (as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the position of fluorescent peaks almost had no change with the increase of excitation wavelength from 290 nm to 350 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength was at 320 nm associated with the strongest emission peak at around 440 nm. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the N, S co-doped CDs was measured to be 17.5% using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54) as a reference.

To evaluate the stability of synthesized N, S co-doped CDs, some test experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. S2a, 4 h of UV irradiation had no significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs. Fig. S2b showed that the effect of different concentrations of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N, S co-doped CDs. It could be observed that the fluorescence intensity of N, S co-doped CDs almost had no obvious change under different concentrations. Simultaneously, Fig. S2c indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs slightly reduced before pH 7, but it remained substantially unchanged after pH 7. Besides, Fig. S2d displayed that there was little change of fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs after storage for 3 month under 4 °C and redispersion in aqueous solution after dried in a vacuum oven. Therefore, the above results indicated that the prepared N, S co-doped CDs possess remarkable optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the N, S co-doped CDs for temperature sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the influence of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs were obtained under different temperatures. The temperature was regularly changed between 10 °C and 70 °C, and the time interval of each measurement was set as 5 min to guarantee the temperature stability of the solution. Interestingly, the prepared N, S co-doped CDs showed good temperature-dependent fluorescence under different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the values of F/F0 gradually decreased with the increase of temperature during the heating process. The regression equation was F/F0 = −0.0058 T + 1.0597 (R2 = 0.9944). While the values increased with the decrease of temperature during the cooling process. The regression equation was F/F0 = -0.0055 T + 1.0456 (R2 = 0.9962). Where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the CDs under elevated temperatures and 10 °C. It can be observed that the fluorescence of N, S co-doped CDs gradually weaken or strengthen with the increase or decrease of temperature in the inset of Fig. 6a. Furthermore, Fig. 6b showed that the fluorescence intensity of the N, S co-doped CDs was reversible during consecutive heating and cooling cycles over the temperature range 10–70 °C. According some reports [41,42], the good temperature-sensitive fluorescence properties may be resulted from photoexcited electron relaxation, which consists of nonradiative relaxation, radiative relaxation and thermally activated trapping in surface and impurity states. The results suggested that the prepared N, S co-doped CDs have great potential for temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the N, S co-doped CDs for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of N, S co-doped CDs were quenched with the addition of TC. In order to achieve the best response between N, S co-doped CDs and TC, the pH value, incubation time were investigated. Generally, pH played important roles the in fluorescence detection. Sometimes it not only affects the fluorescence of the CDs itself, but also affects the reaction of the CDs and the targets. Fig. 7 a exhibited the influence of the different various pH (2.0–10.0) on the value of F/F0. When TC is present, the values of F/F0 would change with pH. The value of F/F0 decreased gradually to a minimum value with the increase of pH up to pH 7.0 and later increased. This tendency revealed that the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs have the best response to TC. Therefore, pH 7.0 was applied for the following explorations. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence quenching reaches stability within 1 min, which achieved the rapid detection of TC.

Under optimized conditions, the N, S co-doped CDs were applied for the detection of TC. The emission spectra of N, S co-doped CDs (Ex = 320 nm) in the presence of varying concentrations of TC were recorded. As shown in Fig. 7c, with the addition of TC, a gradual decline of emission intensity at 440 nm could be observed, which suggested that the fluorescence of N, S co-doped CDs solution was sensitive to TC. Fig. 7d showed that a good linear relationship existed between the values of F/F0 and varies concentrations of TC in the range of 0.1–65 μM. The regression equation is F/F0= -0.0091c + 0.9646 (R2 = 0.9916) with a detection limit of 0.04 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), where c is the concentration of TC, F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensities of the N, S co-doped CDs in the presence and absence of TC, respectively. A comparison was made between currently available methods and the proposed method, which was shown in Table 1 . The results showed that the proposed method is superior with the advantages of low detection limit.

The fluorescence intensity of N, S co-doped CDs decreased after the addition of TC. This phenomenon of fluorescence quenching suggested that there may be some interaction between the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs and TC. FTIR and XPS have indicated that the existence of hydrophilic groups(−OH, −COOH, -SH and -NH2) on the surface of the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs, while there were some hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in TC molecules. Hydrogen bonds would be formed as long as they were mixed. The formation of hydrogen bonds between the N, S co-doped CDs and TC made the CDs target TC with high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of TC. In addition, the UV–vis spectrum of TC and the excitation and emission spectra of the N, S co-doped CDs were recorded. Results were shown in Fig. 8 a. It could be observed that the absorption bands of TC were obvious at 270 and 357 nm and overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of the N, S co-doped CDs. Therefore, the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism may be induced by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). In order to further discriminate the FRET and IFE, the fluorescence lifetime was measured. As shown in Fig. 8b, the fluorescence lifetime of N, S co-doped CDs remained unchanged after the addition of TC, which indicated that the mechanism for TC induced fluorescence quenching is IFE rather than FRET [48]. Therefore, the IFE was considered as one major process in the fluorescence quenching.

Due to the complexity of a real environmental system, the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs should not only show high sensitivity but also selectivity for the TC. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the N, S co-doped CDs to TC, many possible interfering substances including gentamicin, roxithromycin, azithromycin, penicillin, clarithromycin, amoxicillin, AA, Leu, Ala, Tyr, Cys, Glu, Asp, Try, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ were added into the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence of N, S co-doped CDs were quenched while these possible interfering substances almost have no significant influence on the fluorescence. The results indicated the as-prepared N, S co-doped CDs have high selectivity for the detection of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the analytical application of the proposed method in real sample analysis, the developed approach was employed to detect TC in milk samples. The milk was pretreated according to the previous steps and then used for analysis. Recovery experiments were carried out by adding different concentrations to the milk samples. The results were showed in Table 2 . The recoveries of TC were in the range of 97.0–102.5%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower to 5.5%. These results revealed that the proposed method had good accuracy and precision for the detection of TC in milk and had great application prospect.

In summary, N, S co-doped CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method from glutathione without adding the other sources of N and S. The synthesized N, S co-doped CDs showed excellent fluorescence properties, water solubility and stability. Simultaneously, the CDs showed good temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensational linear response from 10 to 70 °C, which indicated the great potential of the N, S co-doped CDs for temperature sensing. Based on the IFE process between the CDs and TC, a simple method was established for the highly selective, sensitive and rapid detection of TC in milk samples using the prepared N, S co-doped CDs as fluorescent probes without further chemical modification. The proposed method offered a good linear relationship between the F/F0 and the different concentrations of TC in the range of 0.1–65 μM with a detection limit of 0.04 μM. Importantly, the successful application of this method in the detection of TC in milk samples revealed that the method has great potential for rapid detection of TC.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was strongly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2017M20162118092).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.08.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic oxidation of the antibiotic tetracycline on TiO 2 and ZnO suspensions
Quinolones and tetracyclines in aquaculture fish by a simple and rapid LC-MS/MS method
Comparison of two fabricated aptasensors based on modified carbon paste/oleic acid and magnetic bar carbon paste/Fe3O4@oleic acid nanoparticle electrodes for tetracycline detection
Determination of tetracycline residues in lake water by on-line coupling of molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction with high performance liquid chromatography
Residue analysis of tetracyclines and their metabolites in eggs and in the environment by HPLC coupled with a microbiological assay and tandem mass spectrometry
Determination of trace tetracyclines in surface water by aluminum hydroxide coprecipitation coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography
Electrochemical determination of tetracycline using AuNP-coated molecularly imprinted overoxidized polypyrrole sensing interface
The determination of tetracycline residues in food using a disposable screen-printed gold electrode (SPGE)
Rapid determination of tetracycline in milk by FT-MIR and FT-NIR spectroscopy
A simple kinetic spectrophotometric method for simultaneous determination of tetracyclines by use of chemometrics
One-pot synthesis of high fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their applications as probes for detection of tetracyclines
Development of fluorescent microgel thermometers based on thermo-responsive polymers and their modulation of sensitivity range
A dual fluorescence temperature sensor based on perylene/exciplex interconversion
One-step synthesis of multi-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric temperature sensing
Fluorescent N-Doped carbon dots as in vitro and in vivo nanothermometer
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from Hongcaitai for selective detection of hypochlorite and mercuric ions and cell imaging
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Microwave assisted green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Dual functional N- and S-co-doped carbon dots as the sensor for temperature and Fe 3+ ions
Microwave assisted synthesis of doped carbon dots and their application as green and simple turn off-on fluorescent sensor for mercury (II) and iodide in environmental samples
Label-Free Carbon-Dots-Based Ratiometric Fluorescence pH Nanoprobes for Intracellular pH Sensing
Photoluminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as temperature probes
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Facile, green and clean one-step synthesis of carbon dots from wool: application as a sensor for glyphosate detection based on the inner filter effect
A portable synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid based on inner filter effect
Ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of chromium(VI) and sulfites
A highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Highly fluorescent N,S-co-doped carbon dots: synthesis and multiple applications
Photophysical properties of doped carbon dots (N, P, and B) and their influence on Electron/Hole transfer in carbon dots–Nickel (II) phthalocyanine conjugates
An "on-off-on" fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot
Facile synthesis of N, B-doped carbon dots and their application for multisensor and cellular imaging
A novel fluorescent carbon dots derived from tamarind
Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe 3+ detection and cell imaging
Comparison of two fabricated aptasensors based on modified carbon paste/oleic acid and magnetic bar carbon paste/Fe 3 O 4 @oleic acid nanoparticle electrodes for tetracycline detection
An efficient fluorescent probe for fluazinam using N, S co-doped carbon dots from L-cysteine
Facile synthesis the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions
One-step spontaneous synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with thermosensitivity from polyethylene glycol
Simultaneous determination of temperature and erlotinib by novel carbon-based sensitive nanoparticles
An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
One-pot carbonization synthesis of europium-doped carbon quantum dots for highly selective detection of tetracycline
An automated salting-out assisted liquid-liquid microextraction approach using 1-octylamine: On-line separation of tetracycline in urine samples followed by HPLC-UV determination
Simultaneous screening and determination of eight tetracycline antibiotics illegally adulterated in herbal preparations using HPLC–DAD combined with LC–MS–MS
A portable synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of chlorogenic acid based on inner filter effect
